one_line_takeaway: ProtoGuard-SL turns raw split-learning embeddings into class-prototype cosine-similarity vectors and filters outliers with a class-conditional conformal test, cutting backdoor ASR to roughly 3-8% while holding or improving clean accuracy across CIFAR-10, SVHN and Bank Marketing against VILLAIN, SplitNN and BadVFL attacks, outperforming DP, model pruning, ANP and VFLIP baselines that traded much more accuracy for lower ASR.
key_finding: On the main comparison (Table I), ProtoGuard-SL achieves the best or near-best MA while sharply cutting ASR across all 3 datasets x 3 attacks (9 settings). Representative numbers: CIFAR-10/VILLAIN: no-defense MA 0.80/ASR 0.92 vs ProtoGuard-SL MA 0.83/ASR 0.06; CIFAR-10/SplitNN: 0.77/0.95 -> 0.85/0.05; CIFAR-10/BadVFL: 0.79/0.89 -> 0.84/0.03; SVHN/VILLAIN: 0.83/0.95 -> 0.87/0.05; SVHN/SplitNN: 0.80/0.95 -> 0.85/0.07; SVHN/BadVFL: 0.78/0.94 -> 0.85/0.04; Bank Marketing/VILLAIN: 0.84/0.95 -> 0.85/0.08; Bank Marketing/SplitNN: 0.82/0.93 -> 0.82/0.05; Bank Marketing/BadVFL: 0.84/0.90 -> 0.86/0.07. The strongest baseline, VFLIP, lowers ASR to roughly 0.27-0.39 range across settings but at the cost of a noticeable MA drop (e.g. down to 0.65-0.76), i.e. the paper's central claim is that ProtoGuard-SL avoids the utility-robustness trade-off other defenses incur. The ablation (Table II, CIFAR-10) shows removing the consistency representation causes the largest ASR increase (e.g. VILLAIN ASR 0.06 -> 0.41), identified by the authors as the most critical component. The architecture study (Table III) shows the effect holds with ResNet-18 and VGG-19 bottom models (ProtoGuard-SL: MA 0.88/ASR 0.05 and MA 0.87/ASR 0.04 respectively, vs no-defense MA 0.86/ASR 0.90 and MA 0.83/ASR 0.87). The alpha sweep (Table IV) shows alpha=0.5 as the reported sweet spot; alpha=0.3 is too permissive (ASR 0.46-0.69), alpha=0.9 over-filters and tanks MA (down to 0.52-0.57) without further ASR benefit.
core_contribution: ProtoGuard-SL is a server-side backdoor defense for vertical split learning (SL) that detects and removes poisoned client embeddings without needing raw features or client-side cooperation. It works in two stages: (1) Prototype Consistency Representation -- the server computes a robust per-class prototype as the coordinate-wise median of embeddings belonging to that class, then re-represents every embedding as a vector of cosine similarities to all class prototypes (a 'consistency vector'), turning absolute embedding geometry into a relational, class-aware representation; (2) Class-conditional, distribution-free conformal filtering -- for each class, a median reference consistency pattern is computed, a deviation score s_k = ||v_k - mu_{y_k}||_2 is computed per embedding, and a conformal p-value (rank of s_k among same-class scores) is used to flag and drop embeddings with p_k <= alpha as poisoned before the top model is trained on them. The authors motivate the design with a visualization (Fig. 1) showing that poisoned and benign embeddings are nearly inseparable in raw embedding space under VILLAIN and SplitNN attacks, but become clearly separable once transformed into the prototype-consistency space.
threat_model: Vertical split learning with n clients holding disjoint feature subsets of the same samples and a server that holds labels and trains the top model. One of the clients (default: 1 of 4) is malicious and performs a poisoning-based, representation-level backdoor attack by manipulating the intermediate embeddings it sends to the server during training (poison rate 5%, poisoning introduced at round 20 of 80, trigger magnitude 1.0), aiming to implant a hidden trigger so the server's top model misclassifies triggered test inputs into an attacker-chosen class while behaving normally otherwise. The defense is deployed entirely server-side and assumes the server has access to per-sample labels (native to SL) but not to clients' raw features or local models. Evaluated attacks are VILLAIN, the SplitNN backdoor attack (He et al.), and BadVFL.
limitations: The paper's own text and design surface several unaddressed limitations: (1) the conformal filtering threshold alpha is a hand-tuned hyperparameter whose optimum (0.5) was found empirically per this experimental setup, and the sensitivity table shows performance is fairly fragile to it, with no adaptive/automatic selection mechanism proposed; (2) the security discussion is a single unproven sentence ('under certain constraints, we can guarantee the security... does not leak sensitive information... thereby ensuring overall system security') with no stated constraints, proof, or privacy analysis -- this is an assertion, not a demonstrated guarantee; (3) the class-prototype and consistency-vector construction assumes the server can reliably estimate per-class benign structure via the coordinate-wise median, which itself degrades if the malicious-client fraction or poison rate is large enough to corrupt a majority of a class's embeddings -- the paper only tests one malicious client out of four and a fixed 5% poison rate, with malicious-client-rate sensitivity relegated to a supplementary file not present in the 22-page excerpt extracted here; (4) all evaluations use small, uniform architectures (4-layer FCN bottom, 3-layer FCN top, with a limited ResNet-18/VGG-19 add-on) and only 3 datasets, one of which (Bank Marketing) is tabular rather than a genuinely 'diverse' benchmark set; (5) no comparison against SafeSplit (NDSS 2025) was performed -- the paper states it was excluded as incompatible (targets U-shaped SL) rather than adapted, so a directly relevant recent defense is left out of the main results table; (6) the defense's robustness against adaptive attackers who know the prototype-consistency transformation and try to mimic benign consistency vectors is not evaluated -- all attacks tested (VILLAIN, SplitNN backdoor, BadVFL) are static/adaptive to the original embedding space, not adaptive to ProtoGuard-SL itself.

datasets_used: Bank Marketing (Moro et al. 2014 bank telemarketing dataset)
